evidence_quotes.quote: as it stands now, it appears that companies are allowed to, at a whim, remove access to games people have bought.

paragraphs.text: I would like to add my support for extending the Digital Fairness Act to also cover bought video/computer games. As it stands now, it appears that companies are allowed to, at a whim, remove access to games people have bought. The most obvious case in recent time is the company "Ubisoft" removing access to the game "The Crew" from people who purchased it. This does not sit right with me and many alongside me, as is evident by the initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames" that have received over 1.4 million signatures in order to prevent similar practices in the future. However, in my opinion, games that have been bought should already be protected from these kinds of acts. Even though I am not personally affected by the removal of "The Crew", I can only assume that my favorite games will follow the same path in the future if nothing is done. When I purchased the games, I was not aware that my ownership could be removed so easily, just because they are considered digital. When I was younger, I bought many games on physical media which I still have to this day because I own them. And I can still play them. The same should be true for my digital games collection. I paid for them. I should own them. If they can be taken away at any point, then I am not owning the games! And most games today are only sold digitally, so I can't even go around the problem and buy it as a physical copy. And even if stores are selling "physical copies", the box only contain some sort of download code for me the download the game digitally anyway. Paying for a product should mean owning a product. Therefore, I hope that the Digital Fairness Act will also include video/computer games. Thank you.
